questions.question: In Minimum Distance Classifier, the distance metric used for prototype matching is typically Euclidean distance. What is the Euclidean distance formula?
questions.explanation: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in a Cartesian coordinate system.

questions.question: A Minimum Distance Classifier uses the closest match between the test image and the training images to classify the pattern. What is the main advantage of this approach?
questions.explanation: The main advantage of Minimum Distance Classifier is that it can handle noisy images and still produce accurate classification results.

questions.question: In Minimum Distance Classifier, if two prototypes have the same minimum distance from the test image, what happens?
options: A) The test image is classified as both patterns
B) The test image is classified as a new pattern
C) The test image is classified as a tie
D) The test image is classified as none of the above
questions.explanation: If two prototypes have the same minimum distance from the test image, the test image is classified as a tie, and the classifier may use heuristics to break the tie.

questions.question: A Minimum Distance Classifier uses a training set of N prototypes. What is the time complexity of this algorithm?
questions.explanation: The time complexity of a Minimum Distance Classifier is O(N^2), where N is the number of prototypes in the training set.

questions.question: A Minimum Distance Classifier uses a distance metric to measure the distance between the test image and each prototype. What is the distance metric used in the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm?
questions.explanation: The distance metric used in the k-Nearest Neighbors algorithm is typically Euclidean distance or Manhattan distance.
